([Mohave](/wiki/Mohave_language "Mohave language"): *eecheeyara tav* [[eːt͡ʃeːjara tav]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA"), also known as *Yara tav*, *Yarate:va*, *Arateve*; c. 1814 – 1874) was a leader of the [Mohave Nation](/wiki/Mohave_people "Mohave people"), known as a mediator between the Mohave and the United States. He was born near the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River") in present-day [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona"). Irataba was a renowned [orator](/wiki/Orator "Orator") and one of the first Mohave to speak English, a skill he used to develop relations with the United States.

Leader of the Mohave Nation (c. 1814 – 1874)

**Irataba** ([Mohave](/wiki/Mohave_language "Mohave language"): *eecheeyara tav* [[eːt͡ʃeːjara tav]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA"), also known as *Yara tav*, *Yarate:va*, *Arateve*; c. 1814 – 1874) was a leader of the [Mohave Nation](/wiki/Mohave_people "Mohave people"), known as a mediator between the Mohave and the United States. He was born near the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River") in present-day [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona"). Irataba was a renowned [orator](/wiki/Orator "Orator") and one of the first Mohave to speak English, a skill he used to develop relations with the United States.

Irataba first encountered [European Americans](/wiki/European_American "European American") in 1851, when he assisted the [Sitgreaves Expedition](/wiki/Sitgreaves_Expedition "Sitgreaves Expedition"). In 1854, he met [Amiel Whipple](/wiki/Amiel_Weeks_Whipple "Amiel Weeks Whipple"), then leading an expedition crossing the Colorado. Several Mohave aided the group, and Irataba agreed to escort them through the territory of the [Paiute](/wiki/Southern_Paiute "Southern Paiute") to the [Old Spanish Trail](/wiki/Old_Spanish_Trail_(trade_route) "Old Spanish Trail (trade route)"), which would take them to southern California. Noted for his large physical size and gentle demeanor, he later helped and protected other expeditions, earning him a reputation among whites as the most important native leader in the region.

Against Irataba's advice, in 1858 Mohave warriors attacked the first emigrant [wagon train](/wiki/Wagon_train "Wagon train") to use [Beale's Wagon Road](/wiki/Beale%27s_Wagon_Road "Beale's Wagon Road") through Mohave country. As a result, the [U.S. War Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_War "United States Department of War") sent a detachment under [Colonel William Hoffman](/wiki/William_Hoffman_(United_States_Army) "William Hoffman (United States Army)") to pacify the tribe. Following a series of confrontations known as the [Mohave War](/wiki/Mohave_War "Mohave War"), Hoffman succeeded in dominating the natives, and demanded that they allow the passage of settlers through their territory. To ensure compliance, [Fort Mohave](/wiki/Fort_Mohave "Fort Mohave") was constructed near the site of the battle in April 1859. Hoffman also imprisoned several Mohave leaders. Having been an advocate for friendly relations with the whites, Irataba became the nation's *Aha macave yaltanack*, an elected, as opposed to hereditary, leader.

As a result of his many interactions with U.S. officials and settlers, Irataba was invited to [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), in 1864, for an official meeting with members of the U.S. military and its government, including President [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln"). In doing so, he became the first Native American from the Southwest to meet an American president. He received considerable attention during his tours of the U.S. capital, and of New York City and Philadelphia, where he was given gifts, including a silver-headed cane from Lincoln. Upon his return he negotiated the creation of the [Colorado River Indian Reservation](/wiki/Colorado_River_Indian_Reservation "Colorado River Indian Reservation"), which caused a split in the Mohave Nation when he led several hundred of his supporters to the Colorado River valley. The majority of the Mohave preferred to remain in their ancestral homelands near Fort Mohave and under the leadership of their hereditary leader, [Homoseh quahote](/wiki/Homoseh_quahote "Homoseh quahote"), who was less enthusiastic about direct collaboration with whites. As leader of the Colorado River band of Mohave, Irataba encouraged peaceful relations with whites, served as a mediator between the warring tribes in the area, and during his later years continued to lead the Mohave in their ongoing conflicts with the [Paiute](/wiki/Paiute "Paiute") and [Chemehuevi](/wiki/Chemehuevi "Chemehuevi").

As noted by ethnographer [Lorraine Sherer](/w/index.php?title=Lorraine_Sherer&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lorraine Sherer (page does not exist)"), "to some he is an heroic figure, to others he was a white collaborator who did not stand up for Mojave rights."[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer196630–31-1) The Irataba Society, a non-profit charity run by the [Colorado River Indian Tribes](/wiki/Colorado_River_Indian_Tribes "Colorado River Indian Tribes"), was established in 1970 in [Parker, Arizona](/wiki/Parker,_Arizona "Parker, Arizona"), where a sports venue, [Irataba Hall](/w/index.php?title=Irataba_Hall&action=edit&redlink=1 "Irataba Hall (page does not exist)"), is also named after him. In 2002, the US [Bureau of Land Management](/wiki/Bureau_of_Land_Management "Bureau of Land Management") designated 32,745 acres (132.51 km2) in the [Eldorado Mountains](/wiki/Eldorado_Mountains "Eldorado Mountains") as [Ireteba Peaks Wilderness](/wiki/Ireteba_Peaks_Wilderness "Ireteba Peaks Wilderness"). In March 2015, Mohave Tribal chairman [Dennis Patch](/w/index.php?title=Dennis_Patch&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dennis Patch (page does not exist)") credited Irataba with ensuring that "the Mohaves stayed on land they had lived on since time immemorial."[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGutekunst2015-2)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irataba&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

[![A lithograph of a man wearing a loincloth and holding a bow in one hand and an arrow in the other](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/ce/Irataba_by_Balduin_M%C3%B6llhausen%2C_1857.jpg)](/wiki/File:Irataba_by_Balduin_M%C3%B6llhausen,_1857.jpg)

Irataba in 1857[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Irataba's name, also rendered as Ireteba, Yara tav, Arateve, Yarate:va, and Yiratewa, derives from the [Mohave language](/wiki/Mojave_language "Mojave language") phrase *eecheeyara tav*, which means "beautiful bird".[[4]](#cite_note-4) He was born into the *Neolge*, or Sun Fire clan of the [Mohave Nation](/wiki/Mohave_people "Mohave people") c. 1814.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicky1999100-5)[[a]](#cite_note-6) He lived near a rock formation that gave its name to [Needles](/wiki/Needles,_California "Needles, California"), south of where the [Grand Canyon](/wiki/Grand_Canyon "Grand Canyon") empties into the [Mohave Canyon](/wiki/Mohave_Canyon "Mohave Canyon") in present-day Arizona, near the Nevada and California border.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeber1925725–727-7) The Mohave lived in houses along the riverbank in the [Mohave Valley](/wiki/Mohave_Valley "Mohave Valley"), during winter in half-buried dwellings built with [cottonwood](/wiki/Populus_sect._Aigeiros "Populus sect. Aigeiros") logs and [arrowweed](/wiki/Arrowweed "Arrowweed") covered in earth, and in the summer in open-air flat-roofed houses called *[ramadas](/wiki/Ramada_(shelter) "Ramada (shelter)")*.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoratto2014347-8)

In the mid-19th century, the Mohave were composed of three geographical groups; Irataba was the hereditary leader of the *Huttoh Pah* group, who lived near the east bank of the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River") and occupied the central portion of the [Mohave Valley](/wiki/Mohave_Valley "Mohave Valley").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer19666,_29–30-9) Mohave government consisted of a loose system of hereditary clan leaders with a head of the entire nation.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer196629–30-10) They were often involved in conflicts with the [Chemehuevi](/wiki/Chemehuevi "Chemehuevi"), [Paiute](/wiki/Southern_Paiute "Southern Paiute"), and [Maricopa peoples](/wiki/Maricopa_people "Maricopa people").[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStewart1971431–434-11) Irataba was a member of the Mohave warrior society called *kwanami*, who led groups of warriors in battle and were dedicated to defending their lands and people.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeber1965177–178-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13)

Little is known of Irataba's family relations, except for the name of his son Tekse thume, and his nephews Qolho qorau (Irataba's sister's son who succeeded him as leader) and Aspamekelyeho.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeberKroeber197325-14) [Olive Oatman](/wiki/Olive_Oatman "Olive Oatman"), who lived with the Mohave for five years, later stated that Irataba was the brother of the former chief, presumably Cairook, with whom Irataba clearly had a close relationship.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGinty2005176-15) One anecdotal description states that Irataba had several wives, among them a [Hualapai](/wiki/Hualapai "Hualapai") woman who had been taken as a captive and who is also described as having a young son.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_Daily_Democrat1891-16) He also had at least one daughter, the mother of his granddaughter Tcatc who was interviewed in the 1950s. She stated that Irataba had wanted to leave his land deeds and medals to his brother's sons, but that they were eventually lost.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDevereux1951-17)

In contemporary accounts Irataba was described as an eloquent speaker, and linguist [Leanne Hinton](/wiki/Leanne_Hinton "Leanne Hinton") suggests that he was among the first Mohave people to become fluent in English, which he learned through his many interactions with Anglo-Americans.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinton1984281-18) Like many Mohave men, Irataba was very tall, particularly by 19th-century standards; the United States Army estimated his height at 6 feet 4 inches (193 cm) in 1861.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIves186169-19) American author [Albert S. Evans](/wiki/Albert_S._Evans "Albert S. Evans"), writing in *[The Overland Monthly](/wiki/Overland_Monthly "Overland Monthly")*, referred to him as "the old desert giant".[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans1869143-20) Edward Carlson, a soldier based at [Fort Mohave](/wiki/Fort_Mohave "Fort Mohave") who knew Irataba well in the 1860s, described him as having a powerful frame, but also a "very gentle" and "kind ... demeanor".[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMifflin2009178-21)

Irataba lived through a tumultuous period of Mohave history where the people went from being a politically independent nation to coming under the political control of the United States, and the events surrounding his role in these encounters are well documented. Most historical sources for the life of Irataba come from descriptions by white explorers or government agents with whom he interacted, or from contemporary newspapers that reported on his visits to the East Coast and California, and on the conflicts in Arizona territory. Some Mohave versions of the events also exist: in the early 20th century anthropologist [A. L. Kroeber](/wiki/A._L._Kroeber "A. L. Kroeber") interviewed Jo Nelson (Mohave: Chooksa homar), a Mohave man who participated in many of the events and knew Irataba personally;[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeberKroeber1973-22) another version was told to [ethnographer](/wiki/Ethnographer "Ethnographer") [George Devereux](/wiki/George_Devereux "George Devereux") by Irataba's granddaughter Tcatc,[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDevereux1951-17) and versions recounted by members of the Fort Mohave band of Mohave, the descendants of Homoseh quahote, were recorded by ethnographer [Lorraine Sherer](/w/index.php?title=Lorraine_Sherer&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lorraine Sherer (page does not exist)") during the 1950s and 1960s.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer1966-23)

## Contact with emigrants and explorers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irataba&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Contact with emigrants and explorers")]

[![A black and white picture of a man with a moustache](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Amiel_Weeks_Whipple.jpg/250px-Amiel_Weeks_Whipple.jpg)](/wiki/File:Amiel_Weeks_Whipple.jpg)

Amiel Weeks Whipple

[![A drawing of some people trading goods near a river](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Whipple_with_the_Mohave.jpg/250px-Whipple_with_the_Mohave.jpg)](/wiki/File:Whipple_with_the_Mohave.jpg)

A drawing from the Whipple Expedition (1856) by [H. Balduin Möllhausen](/wiki/Balduin_M%C3%B6llhausen "Balduin Möllhausen")

Irataba assisted Captain [Lorenzo Sitgreaves](/wiki/Lorenzo_Sitgreaves "Lorenzo Sitgreaves") during his 1851 [exploration of the Colorado](/wiki/Sitgreaves_Expedition "Sitgreaves Expedition").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicky1999100-5) On March 19, 1851, a family of [Brewsterite](/wiki/Church_of_Christ_(Brewsterite) "Church of Christ (Brewsterite)") settlers, the Oatmans, who were traveling by wagon train in what is now Arizona, were [attacked](/wiki/Oatman_massacre "Oatman massacre") by a band of Western [Yavapai](/wiki/Yavapai_people "Yavapai people"), probably the [Tolkepaya](/wiki/Tolkepaya "Tolkepaya").[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBraatz2003253–254-24) They captured two survivors of the attack: the 14-year-old [Olive Oatman](/wiki/Olive_Oatman "Olive Oatman") and her 7-year-old sister, Mary Ann. After a year with the Yavapai, the girls were sold to the Mohave, and adopted into the Oach clan where they lived with the family of a Mohave man called Tokwatha (Musk Melon) a friend of Irataba. Olive remained with the Mohave until February 22, 1856, when Tokwatha, having been warned that having a white girl among them might be seen as an offense by whites, brought her to [Fort Yuma](/wiki/Fort_Yuma "Fort Yuma") carpenter [Henry Grinnell](/wiki/Henry_Grinnell "Henry Grinnell"), releasing her in return for two horses and some blankets and beads.[[24]](#cite_note-25) Irataba may have learned about Anglo-Americans and their society from Oatman, and knowing her may have contributed to his generally favourable disposition towards Whites.

### Whipple Expedition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irataba&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Whipple Expedition")]

On February 23, 1854, Irataba, [Cairook](/wiki/Cairook "Cairook"), and other Mohave people encountered [an expedition](/wiki/Whipple_Expedition "Whipple Expedition") led by military officers [Amiel Whipple](/wiki/Amiel_Weeks_Whipple "Amiel Weeks Whipple") and [J.C. Ives](/wiki/Joseph_Christmas_Ives "Joseph Christmas Ives"), as the group approached the Colorado en route to California.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhippleIves1856112-26)[[b]](#cite_note-30) Whipple and his men counted six hundred Mohave gathered near their camp, trading corn, beans, [squash (plant)](/wiki/Squash_(plant) "Squash (plant)"), and wheat for beads and [calico](/wiki/Calico "Calico").[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhippleIves1856113–114-31) By the end of their commerce, the party had purchased six bushels of corn and two hundred pounds of flour.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhippleIves1856114-32) The Mohave played a traditional game played with a hoop and pole, and the two groups entertained themselves with target practice, the Mohave using bows and arrows and the whites firing pistols and rifles.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhippleIves1856114-32) When the expedition had difficulty crossing the Colorado on February 27, several Mohave jumped into the water and helped salvage the supplies.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhippleIves1856116-33)

Irataba and Cairook agreed to escort the group across the territory of the Paiute to the [Old Spanish Trail](/wiki/Old_Spanish_Trail_(trade_route) "Old Spanish Trail (trade route)") that would take them to southern California.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhippleIves1856119–128-34) German artist [Balduin Möllhausen](/wiki/Balduin_M%C3%B6llhausen "Balduin Möllhausen") accompanied the Whipple Expedition, and made drawings of several Mohave, including Irataba. Möllhausen's drawings were featured in Ives' 1861 congressional report, making Irataba "among the first *named* likenesses of California Indians ever published".[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEElsasser197762-35)

### Ives Expedition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irataba&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ives Expedition")]

In February 1858, Ives returned to the area in a [paddle steamer](/wiki/Paddle_steamer "Paddle steamer") named *Explorer*. He was leading an expedition up the Colorado from the south, and he wanted Irataba to guide them.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMifflin2009174-36) The Mohave gave permission to navigate the river, and Cairook, Irataba, and a 16-year-old Mohave boy named Nahvahroopa joined them.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIves186169–71,_76-37) Möllhausen again accompanied the expedition, and was impressed with the Mohave guides, later noting Irataba's enthusiastic handshake and lamenting that their only form of communication was sign language. He also noted that Irataba and the Mohave readily began wearing European clothes given to them by members of the expedition, and showed great interest in [smoking tobacco](/wiki/Smoking_tobacco "Smoking tobacco").[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1972178–179,_182-38) Aside from the friendship shown to him by Irataba and Cairook, Ives noted that the Mohave appeared less friendly than on earlier occasions, a change that he attributed to their contact with [Mormons](/wiki/Mormon "Mormon"), who were in conflict with the US and had succeeded in converting some Mohave.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMifflin2009175-39)[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStewart1969230-40)[[c]](#cite_note-42)

Irataba guided the party into the Mohave Canyon, indicating the location of rapids and advising the *Explorer*'s pilot of convenient places to anchor while camping for the night.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIves186177,_85,_92-43) When they reached the entrance to the [Black Canyon of the Colorado](/wiki/Black_Canyon_of_the_Colorado "Black Canyon of the Colorado"), the ship crashed against a submerged rock, throwing several men overboard, dislodging the [boiler](/wiki/Boiler "Boiler"), and damaging the [wheelhouse](/wiki/Bridge_(nautical) "Bridge (nautical)"). Using their [skiff](/wiki/Skiff "Skiff"), the crew towed the *Explorer* to the shore, where they camped for three days while repairing the vessel.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIves186181–84-44) The expedition had relied on beans and corn provided by the Mohave during the previous weeks; as their supplies dwindled they grew increasingly anxious about the arrival of a resupply pack train from Fort Yuma. Irataba volunteered to hike towards the Mohave Valley to try to locate the supplies that had been requested several days earlier. He also warned that the expedition was being watched by Paiutes.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIves186179–83-45)

When Irataba returned he informed Ives that he would not venture any deeper into the territory of the [Hualapais](/wiki/Hualapai "Hualapai"), but agreed to help them locate friendly guides in the region before parting company.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIves186194–97-46) Irataba was reluctant to venture into the canyon because he feared the party would be ambushed by Paiutes aligned with Mormons.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZappia2014121,_138-47) After enlisting three Hualapai guides, Irataba prepared to take his leave from the expedition and return to the Mohave community.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIves186194–97,_102-48) On April 4, the Mohave received payment for their services; Möllhausen described the exchange: "Lieutenant Ives informed Irataba that he had been authorized by the 'Great Grandfather in Washington' to give him two mules ... for his loyalty and his trustworthiness so that he could take his possessions and those of his companions more conveniently to his home valley."[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1972188-49) The next morning, as they were preparing to leave: "Irataba was visibly moved ... and in his sincere eyes expressed so much honesty and loyalty as can only be found in an unspoiled nature ... I maintain that there was not one in our expedition who did not feel a certain sadness to see this huge man with ... a harmless soul leave."[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1972188-49)

## Mohave War and aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irataba&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Mohave War and aftermath")]

Main article: [Mohave War](/wiki/Mohave_War "Mohave War")

Following their experiences with the Sitgreaves and Whipple expeditions, and encounters with Mormons and the soldiers at Fort Yuma, the Mohave were aware that whites were immigrating to the region in increasing numbers. It was difficult for the Mohave to predict the behavior of the arriving whites, some of whom, like Whipple, were amiably disposed towards them, whereas others such as the Mormons were hostile. The kwanami were divided on how the situation should be approached; some advocated an aggressive posture, denying whites all passage through their territory, but others, including Irataba, preferred a peaceful approach, or even an alliance with them that could put the Mohave in a stronger position relative to their traditional enemies the Paiute, Chemehuevi, and Walapai.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeber1965177–178-12)

On September 1, 1857, a joint force of Mohave and Quechan warriors launched a major attack with hundreds of warriors on a Maricopa village close to [Maricopa Wells, Arizona](/wiki/Maricopa_Wells,_Arizona "Maricopa Wells, Arizona"), on the [Gila River](/wiki/Gila_River "Gila River"). The [Battle of Pima Butte](/wiki/Battle_of_Pima_Butte "Battle of Pima Butte") was described by a group of white mail carriers who witnessed it. They stated that the battle was fought mostly with clubs, as well as bows and arrows, and that probably more than 100 warriors died, most of them from the attacking party. It is unknown whether Irataba participated in the attack, but given his status as kwanami, it is likely that he did. The battle has been described as the last major battle involving only Native American nations.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeberFontana1986-50) This defeat put the Mohave on the defensive, wary of the possibility that whites would take this moment of weakness as an opportunity to begin settling on tribal lands.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeber1965175-51)[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStewart1969230-40)

### Rose–Baley Party attack

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irataba&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Rose–Baley Party attack")]

In October 1857, an expedition led by [Edward Fitzgerald Beale](/wiki/Edward_Fitzgerald_Beale "Edward Fitzgerald Beale") was tasked with establishing a trade route along the [35th parallel](/wiki/35th_parallel_north "35th parallel north") from [Fort Smith](/wiki/Fort_Smith,_Arkansas "Fort Smith, Arkansas"), Arkansas, to [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), California.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThrapp199176-52)[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHunter1979139-53) From Fort Smith, his journey continued through [Fort Defiance, Arizona](/wiki/Fort_Defiance,_Arizona "Fort Defiance, Arizona"), before crossing the Colorado near Needles, California.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicky1999100-5) This route became known as [Beale's Wagon Road](/wiki/Beale%27s_Wagon_Road "Beale's Wagon Road"), and the location where Beale crossed the river as Beale's Crossing.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUtley1981164-54) Beale's journal and subsequent report to the [United States Secretary of War](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_War "United States Secretary of War") did not mention any problems with Irataba and the Mohave, but an assistant named Humphrey Stacy recorded that the Mohave had prevented Beale from traveling downriver.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer199469-55)

In 1858, the [Rose–Baley Party](/wiki/Rose%E2%80%93Baley_Party "Rose–Baley Party") comprising at least a hundred settlers with wagons and a large herd of cattle, was the first emigrant train to venture onto Beale's Wagon Road.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHunter1979138-56)[[d]](#cite_note-58) Upon entering Mohave territory the settlers started cutting down cottonwood trees, which were an important resource for the Mohave. The most warlike of the kwanamis organized an attack on the party, and drove away and slaughtered many of the party's cattle.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeber1965174–175-59) On August 30, three hundred Mohave warriors attacked the emigrants but were repelled, with one member of the party killed in battle and eleven wounded. Another family who were not with the main party during the attack were killed in an event that has often been blamed on the Mohave, but which in [Colonel William Hoffman](/wiki/William_Hoffman_(United_States_Army) "William Hoffman (United States Army)")'s opinion was more likely carried out by a band of Walapai, along with seven renegade Mohave.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer199482–86,_95-60) The emigrants killed seventeen Mohave warriors.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHunter1979139-53)[[57]](#cite_note-61) The incident was widely publicized in the media and labeled "a massacre",[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer199482-62) scaring many white Californians who feared being cut off from the eastern US by hostile natives, and it motivated the War Department to quickly subjugate the tribe.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeberKroeber197353–54-63)[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeber1965175-51)

Irataba was away at Fort Yuma during the attack on the settlers, and upon hearing of it he scolded the Mohave. Chooksa homar participated in the events and reported that Irataba told the warriors, "I hear you fought, though I told you not to. And you will have war again: I know it. You used to fight the Maricopa. I want to go [to Phoenix] to see the Maricopa and tell them: 'The Mohave will not come any more to attack you'."[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeberKroeber197314-64) Irataba, weary of the constant fighting and worried that further conflicts with neighboring tribes would draw more attention from the US soldiers, organized a peace expedition to the Maricopa, settling the ancient disputes between the two peoples.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeberKroeber197354–54-65)

### Conflict with the US army

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irataba&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Conflict with the US army")]

[![A black and white sketch of Fort Mohave in the late 19th century](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Fort_Mohave_sketch.gif/250px-Fort_Mohave_sketch.gif)](/wiki/File:Fort_Mohave_sketch.gif)

A sketch of Fort Mohave in the late 19th century

When news of the attack reached California, the [US War Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_War "United States Department of War") decided to establish a military fort at Beale's Crossing to keep the Mohave under control and secure white travelers safe passage through Mohave lands. On December 26, 1858, Hoffman and fifty [dragoons](/wiki/Dragoon "Dragoon") from [Fort Tejon](/wiki/Fort_Tejon "Fort Tejon") were dispatched to cross the desert and confront the Mohave.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195358-66) Irataba attempted to arrange a peaceful meeting, but Hoffman ordered his troops to fire on the warriors, who counterattacked and repelled the force.[[63]](#cite_note-67) He returned in April 1859, by way of Fort Yuma, with four [companies](/wiki/Company_(military_unit) "Company (military unit)") of the [6th Infantry Regiment](/wiki/6th_Infantry_Regiment_(United_States) "6th Infantry Regiment (United States)").[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195358-66) When they arrived at Beale's Crossing, the Mohave decided against attacking the army of five hundred soldiers.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195358-66) On April 23, at Hoffman's request, he and his officers met with several hundred Mohave warriors and their leaders. The indigenous leaders included Cairook, Irataba, [Homoseh quahote](/wiki/Homoseh_quahote "Homoseh quahote") (also called Seck-a-hoot and Asika hota), and Pascual, leader of the Quechan.[[64]](#cite_note-68) During the meeting the Mohave were surrounded by armed soldiers who prevented them from leaving.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHunter1979150-69)

Hoffman gave the Mohave a choice between war and peace, and he demanded that they agree to never again harm white settlers along the wagon trail. He also declared that, as punishment, the Mohave were required to surrender as hostages a member of each clan and three warriors who had taken part in the attack.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHunter1979150-69) Hoffman demanded that the chief who had ordered the attack on the settlers offer himself up as a hostage. According to Chooksa homar, the chief who had ordered the attack was Homoseh quahote, who was reluctant to give himself up. Cairook offered himself in his place, along with eight others, including Tokwatha, Irataba's son Tekse thume, and Irataba's nephews Qolho qorau, and Aspamekelyeho. Other hostages were named Itsere-'itse, Ilyhanapau, and Tinyam-isalye.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeberKroeber197325,_66-70) They were transported in the river steamer *General Jessup* to Fort Yuma.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195359-71) Many soldiers remained to begin construction on the Beale's Crossing fort, which was named Fort Mohave.[[68]](#cite_note-72) Although Irataba visited the garrison several times and argued for their release, the hostages were held for more than a year.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMifflin2009177-73) On June 21, 1859, Cairook and one other captive were killed by soldiers while attempting to escape their incarceration.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195359–60-74) Most of the other Mohave captives escaped.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195359–60-74)[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeberKroeber197327-75)

In July 1861, the commander of Fort Mohave, Major [Lewis Armistead](/wiki/Lewis_Armistead "Lewis Armistead"), ordered soldiers to fire into a group of Mohave who he suspected of having attacked a mail carrier and slaughtered his mule. The Mohave did not respond violently to the attack, but Armistead decided to punish them for harassing the mail party. He arrived at Irataba's ranch where a group of Mohave boys were planting beans, and from a hidden position he shot one of the planters, killing him. This attack prompted an assault on Armistead's detachment, who from an advantageous position on the high ground were able to repel the Mohave, killing many of them in a battle that lasted most of the day. Armistead's report of the assault of Irataba's ranch reported 23 dead Mohave warriors, but the Mohave remember a much higher number of casualties, including women and children killed by the soldiers. Sherer speculated that, given that 50 soldiers fired around 10 shots each, the casualty count could have been much higher than reported. The attack on Irataba's ranch is remembered by the Mohave as "the first and last battle with the Federal Troops."[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer1994103–105-76)

## As *Aha macave yaltanack*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irataba&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: As Aha macave yaltanack")]

[![A wood block depiction of a man](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Chief_Irataba_of_the_Mojave_Nation%2C_February_1864%2C_artist%27s_impression.jpg/250px-Chief_Irataba_of_the_Mojave_Nation%2C_February_1864%2C_artist%27s_impression.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chief_Irataba_of_the_Mojave_Nation,_February_1864,_artist%27s_impression.jpg)

Artist's impression of Irataba, February 1864

After Cairook's death, whites living near the Colorado River began to view Irataba as the main leader of the Mohave.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195360-77) He had become *Aha macave yaltanack* (leader of the Mohave Nation) or *hochoch* (leader elected by the people); "*yaltanack*" is Mohave for "leader", and "*hochach*" means "head of a group".[[74]](#cite_note-78) Homoseh quahote was a hereditary leader of the *Malika* clan ("the understanding people"), and the position of head of the Mohave was traditionally inherited only by someone from that clan. Because Irataba was of the *Huttoh pah*, in order for him to become the leader of the Mohave Nation, Homoseh quahote had to step down so Irataba, as an elected leader, could take his place.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer19663–4-79)

Despite the accepted English translations, the words *yaltanack* and *huchach* do not mean a "ruler" or "boss".[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer19662-80) Devereux describes Mohave government as "one of the least understood segments of Mohave culture", and notes that while white officials "tended to act on the assumption that Indian chiefs exercised absolute authority", as an elected leader Irataba was "primarily a servant of the tribe".[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDevereux195135–36-81)

By the mid-1860s a deep rift had developed between Irataba, who was proactive in cooperating with white settlers, and Homoseh quahote, who passively tolerated but did not approve of white encroachment on Mohave lands.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer19669,_30-82) Irataba was *Aha macave yaltanack* of the Mohave from 1861 to 1866, but from 1867 to 1869 opinions differ, and by 1870 US government correspondence suggests that Homoseh quahote had succeeded him as leader of the Fort Mohave group.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer19661–14-83)

## Mining ventures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irataba&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Mining ventures")]

Probably first to bring national attention to the potential gold in the region was F. X. Aubry, who in 1853 reported finding gold at a crossing of the Colorado and that he traded for legendary gold bullets further east with [Tonto Apache](/wiki/Tonto_Apache "Tonto Apache"). Newspapers across the country copied the gold strike story.[[80]](#cite_note-84) Over the next decade mining discoveries up and down the [lower Colorado River valley](/wiki/Lower_Colorado_River_Valley "Lower Colorado River Valley") brought miners into newly formed mining districts.[[81]](#cite_note-85) In the spring of 1861, Mohave, possibly including Irataba, aided a group of prospectors discover gold in Eldorado Canyon north east of Ft. Mohave, site of first mining rush to the immediate area.[[82]](#cite_note-86) This was followed by the discovery of the San Francisco district (now Oatman district), where Irataba led John Moss to the rich Moss lode.[[83]](#cite_note-87) Earlier, concern for protecting incoming miners brought together tribal leaders to Ft. Yuma in April 1863, including Irataba, where they pledged to allow prospectors into their country. On his return home, the Mohave joined a prospecting party led by mountain man [Joseph R. Walker](/wiki/Joseph_R._Walker "Joseph R. Walker") who had prospected among them on the river in 1860.[[84]](#cite_note-88) In 1863, Irataba acted as a guide for the [Walker Party](/wiki/Walker_Party "Walker Party"), gold prospectors led by Walker and including [Jack Swilling](/wiki/Jack_Swilling "Jack Swilling"), who later founded [Phoenix, Arizona](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona"). Irataba brought them to a river that he called *Hasyamp*, later named the [Hassayampa River](/wiki/Hassayampa_River "Hassayampa River"), where they found plentiful gold. Central Arizona's first mining district was established there the following year, which led to the founding of [Prescott, Arizona](/wiki/Prescott,_Arizona "Prescott, Arizona") soon afterward.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanchett19989-89) Relations between settlers and the Mohave were positive during this period, but as emigration increased, gold seekers founded towns, largest on the river being named [La Paz](/wiki/La_Paz,_Arizona "La Paz, Arizona"), stirring fear among settlers of a native uprising against further encroachment on Mohave land.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195360–61-90) The following year a group of soldiers from Fort Mohave discovered an area between Needles, Fort Mohave, and the Colorado River that was abundant in copper ore. The parcel was named Irataba Mining District, and within the year a mining company had been formed to work it. A small mining town named Irataba City was established on a bluff two miles below Fort Mohave.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShumwayVredenburghHartill198076-91)[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWebbSilliman1974270-92) High copper market prices during the Civil War brought into operation the Bill Williams Fork copper mines as well, which shipped copper ore from the new Colorado River landing of Aubry. Though there would be down turns, mining in the Mohave lands was now well established.

## Travels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irataba&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Travels")]

[![A black and white photograph of Washington, D.C., in April 1865, with the Capital building in the background](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Washington%2C_D.C.%2C_April_1865_34773v.jpg/250px-Washington%2C_D.C.%2C_April_1865_34773v.jpg)](/wiki/File:Washington,_D.C.,_April_1865_34773v.jpg)

Washington, D.C., April 1865

As related above, during the early 1860s, Irataba befriended prospectors as a guide. One of them was John Moss, whom Irataba had shown the location of a gold mine in the [Mojave desert](/wiki/Mojave_Desert "Mojave Desert"), which Moss later sold.[[89]](#cite_note-93)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_Daily_Democrat1891-16) Moss suggested Irataba be invited to Washington so that he could see firsthand the United States' military might.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195363-94)[[e]](#cite_note-96) In November 1863, Irataba traveled with Moss to [San Pedro, Los Angeles](/wiki/San_Pedro,_Los_Angeles "San Pedro, Los Angeles"), where they boarded the steamship *Senator*, bound for San Francisco. In San Francisco, he stayed at the [Occidental Hotel](/wiki/Occidental_Hotel "Occidental Hotel") and created a storm as he walked down [Jackson Street](/wiki/Jackson_Street_(San_Francisco) "Jackson Street (San Francisco)"), dressed in clothing typical of European Americans, which Irataba soon preferred to traditional Mohave clothing.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195361–62-97) The press documented his every movement and wrote extensively about his physical size and strong features.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMifflin2009178-21) On December 2, 1863, the *Daily Evening Bulletin* described Irataba as a large man, "granitic in appearance as one of the Lower Coast mountains, with ... a lower jaw massive enough to crush nuts or crush quartz."[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195361–62-97)

[![A black and white picture of a woman with a tattoo on her chin](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Olive_Oatman%2C_1857.png/250px-Olive_Oatman%2C_1857.png)](/wiki/File:Olive_Oatman,_1857.png)

Olive Oatman, a former resident of the Mohave

In January 1864 they sailed for the [Isthmus of Panama](/wiki/Isthmus_of_Panama "Isthmus of Panama") on board the *Orizaba*, then traveled onwards to [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Brien2006249-98) Upon Irataba's arrival in New York, *[Harper's Weekly](/wiki/Harper%27s_Weekly "Harper's Weekly")* described him as "the finest specimen of unadulterated aboriginal on this continent".[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195353-99) Here Irataba exchanged his suit and sombrero for the uniform and regalia of a [major general](/wiki/Major_general "Major general"), including a bright yellow sash, a gold badge encrusted with precious stones, and a medal with the inscription, "Irataba, Chief of the Mohaves, Arizona Territory".[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195362–63-100) In February, when *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* asked him to explain the nature of his visit, he replied: "to see where so many pale faces come from".[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''The_New_York_Times''1864-101) In New York he met with the former Mohave resident [Olive Oatman](/wiki/Olive_Oatman "Olive Oatman"), and the two conversed in Mohave. Irataba told Oatman that her Mohave adoptive sister Topeka, to whom she had grown very close, still missed her and hoped she would return.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMifflin2009180-102) Oatman described the encounter as a meeting between friends.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMifflin2009180-102)

Irataba moved on to Philadelphia and Washington, D.C., where he earned great acclaim; government officials and military officers lavished him with gifts of medals, swords, and photographs.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195362-103) In Washington he met with President [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln"), who gave him a silver-headed cane.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicky1999101–102-104) He was the first Native American from the Southwest to meet an American president.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarlson1886492-105) The tour ended in April, when he and Moss sailed to California, again by way of Panama, and made their way back to Beale's Crossing from Los Angeles by wagon.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195362–63-100)

Upon Irataba's return from Washington D.C., he met with the Mohave while dressed in his major general's uniform, with medals. He wore a European-style hat and carried a long Japanese sword, and he told the Mohave about all the things he had seen.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195363–64-106) He tried to convince them that peace with the United States was in their best interests, and that war against them was futile, stressing their dominant military capabilities.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicky1999102-107) Many Mohave were skeptical of his reports, and reacted with disbelief.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195366-108)

## Colorado River Indian Reservation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irataba&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Colorado River Indian Reservation")]

Main article: [Colorado River Indian Reservation](/wiki/Colorado_River_Indian_Reservation "Colorado River Indian Reservation")

[![A color map of a section of land near California, Arizona, and Nevada](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/The_Colorado_River_Indian_Reservation_in_Relation_to_Arizona%2C_California_and_Associated_Counties.jpg/250px-The_Colorado_River_Indian_Reservation_in_Relation_to_Arizona%2C_California_and_Associated_Counties.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Colorado_River_Indian_Reservation_in_Relation_to_Arizona,_California_and_Associated_Counties.jpg)

The Colorado River Indian Reservation

The completion of Fort Mohave began the process of military subjugation of the Mohave, and the next step was the establishment of [reservations](/wiki/Indian_reservation "Indian reservation"). In 1863, [Charles Debrille Poston](/wiki/Charles_Debrille_Poston "Charles Debrille Poston"), the first Superintendent of Indian Affairs of the [Arizona Territory](/wiki/Arizona_Territory "Arizona Territory"), called a conference between the Chemehuevi and Irataba's faction of the Mohave, in which he convinced them to form an alliance with the US against the Apache. The treaty was never ratified by the US Congress but formed an important step in establishing friendly relations between the Mohave and the US government following the military campaigns and the establishment of Fort Mohave.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnack200497-109)

Poston promoted the idea of establishing a reservation in the southern portion of Mohave country. Many Mohave opposed the proposed location and instead argued for a smaller parcel further north in the Mohave Valley, which had more fertile land.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer19666–7-110) With Irataba and an engineer, Poston traveled down the Colorado River to survey a location.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaylor2000196-111) In August 1864 the post commander at Fort Mohave, Captain Charles Atchisson, stated that Irataba was against the proposed location. In September 1864, Poston gave the impression that Irataba was in favor of it.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer19667–8-112) In a letter to General Richard C. Drum, Atchisson reported that Irataba and four Mohave leaders were unhappy with how Poston was handling the situation:

> Mr. Poston had marked out a reservation for the Mojave Indians in the upper part of the La Paz valley on the East side of the Colorado River ... Iratabu says this reservation is covered with sand and unfit for cultivation and the Indians are opposed to giving up their good lands in the Mojave Valley and moving to it ... Iratabu says if he can have the valley below the Fort Mojave reserved for the home of the Indians, he is willing to give up all claims to lands on other parts of the river, and bring his Indians from La Paz and other points to this valley ... I have full confidence in the friendship of Iratabu towards white men, but not in his tribe, if troubles with any other Indians should occur, while he has more influence over them than any other chief, his control over them is not complete, and they are as likely to lead him (as he is them).[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer19667–8-112)

[![A black and white photograph showing a group of approximately 150 Native Americans in European clothing standing at the slope of a ditch with an arid desert landscape in the background](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/GROUP_OF_MOHAVE_INDIANS%2C_ARIZONA_-_NARA_-_524280.tif/lossy-page1-250px-GROUP_OF_MOHAVE_INDIANS%2C_ARIZONA_-_NARA_-_524280.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:GROUP_OF_MOHAVE_INDIANS,_ARIZONA_-_NARA_-_524280.tif)

Group of Mohave people during a geographical survey of the Colorado River reservation in 1871

Faced with Irataba's disagreement, Poston promised that the US government would assist the Mohave with installing an irrigation system at the Colorado that would make most of the reservation arable.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaylor2000-113) This apparently convinced Irataba, who traveled to the Colorado River Valley with about 800 people, almost a fifth of the entire Mohave Nation.[[109]](#cite_note-114) General [James Henry Carleton](/wiki/James_Henry_Carleton "James Henry Carleton") thought a reservation was unnecessary, and engineer [Herman Ehrenberg](/wiki/Herman_Ehrenberg "Herman Ehrenberg") disagreed with Poston's proposed location on the basis that the soil was too alkaline for farming, the need for irrigation too great, and the task of raising the river too insurmountable. Nonetheless, on March 3, 1865,[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKappler1904-115) Congress established the [Colorado River Indian Reservation](/wiki/Colorado_River_Indian_Reservation "Colorado River Indian Reservation") at Poston's proposed location.[[111]](#cite_note-116) Ehrenberg's concerns proved valid, and neither Poston nor any subsequent US authority was willing to dedicate the considerable resources required to make the location suitable for farming.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaylor2000-113) The reservation was established without a treaty having been established between the Mohave and the US government.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwanton1952357-117) The boundaries were expanded and clarified by [executive orders](/wiki/Executive_order "Executive order") issued by [President Grant](/wiki/Ulysses_S._Grant "Ulysses S. Grant") in 1873, 1874 and 1867.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwanton1952357-117)[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKappler1904-115)

Most of the Mohave refused to leave their ancestral homelands for the reservation, but Irataba's conviction that the reservation was their best option marked the beginning of a rift between his group and those who stayed behind to follow Homoseh quahote, the nation's hereditary leader.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer19664–5,_8–9-118) According to an eyewitness account by Chooksa homar, Irataba explained his decision to move:

> "You get angry sometimes; I know you are brave men and think you can beat anybody. You thought you could beat the whites: you said so. I told you you could not; the whites have beaten all tribes; all are friends to them now. You did not listen to what I said when I told you that. You did what you thought, and many have got killed. If the soldiers come, you cannot resist them. You did not know that, but now you know it. The country down river from here, which we took away from another tribe [the Halchidhoma], I will live there. Those of you who want to go on fighting can stay here. I do not want to and will leave you."[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeberKroeber197331-119)

As the promised irrigation assistance was not immediately forthcoming, the first year at the reservation brought a drought that made it necessary for the Mohave to request food assistance. In 1867, Irataba and the Mohave began to build an irrigation canal, digging by hand a ditch that ran for 9 miles (14 km).[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaylor2000-113)[[f]](#cite_note-120) A report by a US official visiting the reservation in 1870 recorded that attempts by the Mohave at agricultural cultivation on the site were restricted to an area of not more than 40 acres (16 ha).[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAndrews1870647-121)

## Later years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irataba&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Later years")]

[![A black and white photograph of two Native Americans wearing military-style uniforms](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Ah-oochy_Kah-ma_and_Ireteba.jpg/250px-Ah-oochy_Kah-ma_and_Ireteba.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ah-oochy_Kah-ma_and_Ireteba.jpg)

Irataba (right) with Yavapai leader Ohatchecama, photographed at the trial following the Wickenburg Massacre ([Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress") photograph collection)[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGentile1870-122)

Irataba continued to lead the Colorado River band of Mohave during the 1860s. He pursued peaceful relations with the surrounding tribes and cooperated actively with US authorities. He also helped the Yavapai and Walapai in ongoing conflicts with Paiutes and Chemehuevi.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeber1965176–178-123)

In March 1865, Irataba and the Mohave defeated the Chemehuevi after their allies, the Paiutes, killed two Mohave women.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195364–65-124) The Mohave pushed the Chemehuevi off their traditional territory and into the California desert, but they soon returned.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnack200497–98-125) To avoid fighting a two-front war, Irataba attacked the Chemehuevi first, then turned his attention to the Paiutes, who were planning an attack on the Mohave farm and granary on the Colorado's Cottonwood Island.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''Daily_Alta_California''18651-126) During a subsequent battle with the Chemehuevi in October 1865, Irataba was taken prisoner while wearing his major general's uniform.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNichols1944120–121-127) According to a contemporary news report based on second hand accounts from white travelers, the captors feared that killing him would invite repercussions from the soldiers stationed at Fort Mohave, so they instead stripped him naked and sent him home badly beaten.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''Daily_Alta_California''18651-126) In the Mohave account of the events as told by Chooksa homar, Irataba surrendered in an effort to make peace with the Chemehuevi, and offered his uniform to their chief as a gesture of peace.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeberKroeber197340-128) In 1867, a treaty signed by Irataba and the Chemehuevi leader Pan Coyer restored peaceful relations between the two nations.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeber1965179-129)

> The old man is here now with his tribe, but he looks feeble, wan, and grief stricken. Age has come to Irataba, but it has brought to him no bright and peaceful twilight. Dark and cheerless appear the skies of his declining years.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195367-130)[[g]](#cite_note-132)

—*The [Arizona Weekly Miner](/wiki/Arizona_Weekly_Miner "Arizona Weekly Miner")*, February 5, 1870

Irataba also welcomed bands of Yavapai onto the reservation after they had been subject to massacres by US troops, or suffered starvation due to having been driven from their lands. The meager resources of the reservation proved unable to sustain the additional population and eventually the Yavapai had to leave. Irataba frequently served as a mediator between Yavapai and Walapai who had become embroiled in conflicts with the US army, and participated in peace parlays. In 1871–72, General [George Crook](/wiki/George_Crook "George Crook") came to the Mohave reservation looking for a group of Yavapai thought to be responsible for the [Wickenburg Massacre](/wiki/Wickenburg_Massacre "Wickenburg Massacre"), and Irataba had no choice but to turn the war party over to the army.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeber1965179-129) He traveled to the trial proceedings at [Fort Date Creek](/wiki/Fort_Date_Creek "Fort Date Creek"), where he was instructed to hand tobacco to the Yavapai he believed to be responsible, as a way for him to testify against them without their realizing it. As soldiers attempted to arrest the men that Irataba identified, fighting broke out and one of the Yavapai leaders, [Ohatchecama](/wiki/Ohatchecama "Ohatchecama"), and his brother were shot.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFarish1918-133) Despite having been shot twice and stabbed with a bayonet, Ohatchecama survived and escaped to organize the Tolkepaya Yavapai in resistance against the US army.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBraatz2003112,_135-134) The Yavapai felt betrayed by Irataba, and plotted to kill him in revenge, but were eventually persuaded that he was not the one who had turned them over to Crook.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2007130-135)[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBraatz2003135-136)

The Colorado River band of Mohave never replaced Irataba; he was their leader when he died at their reservation on May 3 or 4, 1874.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodward195367-130) His cause of death is unknown, but [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox") and natural causes are both cited.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERicky1999102-107) The Mohave grieved deeply; Irataba's cremation and the rituals of mourning were reported in newspapers as far away as Omaha, Nebraska.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''The_Omaha_Daily_Bee''18742-137)[[h]](#cite_note-140) Irataba was succeeded as leader on the Colorado Reservation by his nephew, Qolho qorau of the *Vemacka* clan, who upheld his uncle's policies.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer196613-141)

## Legacy and influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irataba&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Legacy and influence")]

[![A color picture of some desert mountains](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Ireteba_Peaks_Wilderness.png/250px-Ireteba_Peaks_Wilderness.png)](/wiki/File:Ireteba_Peaks_Wilderness.png)

Ireteba Peaks Wilderness

[![A color picture of the entry to the Bluewater Resort and Casino, with a sign celebrating the 150th anniversary of the reservation. The logo includes a black and white image of Irataba.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Bluewater_Irataba.jpg/250px-Bluewater_Irataba.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bluewater_Irataba.jpg)

Bluewater Resort and Casino, with image of Irataba

In 1966, Sherer commented regarding Irataba's legacy amongst the Mohave: "Estimation of his position in Mojave history from the Mojave viewpoint differs. To some he is an heroic figure, to others he was a white collaborator who did not stand up for Mojave rights. From the standpoint of white men who were conquering a wilderness, he was indeed the Mojave who worked unswervingly for peace."[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer196630–31-1)

Irataba's influence as a leader may even have left its mark on the [Mohave language](/wiki/Mohave_language "Mohave language"). A list of Mohave words that he dictated to an anthropologist during his visit in Washington shows that he was among the first Mohave speakers to shift the sounds [s] and [ʂ] (similar to sh as in "shack") to [θ] (th as in "thick") and [s], respectively. By the late 19th century all Mohave speakers had adopted this change. Linguist Leanne Hinton has suggested that this may be due in part to Irataba's influence, both because he was a prestigious leader whose ways of speaking may have been emulated by other Mohave, and also because when he led the Mohave onto the reservation the old distinctions between dialect groups were erased through [dialect leveling](/wiki/Dialect_leveling "Dialect leveling"), making new changes spread quickly through the community.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinton1979-142)

The Irataba Society, a non-profit charity run by the [Colorado River Indian Tribes](/wiki/Colorado_River_Indian_Tribes "Colorado River Indian Tribes"), was established in [Parker, Arizona](/wiki/Parker,_Arizona "Parker, Arizona"), in 1970.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKulp19743-143) The charity holds an annual [pow wow](/wiki/Pow_wow "Pow wow") or National Indian Days celebration.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECook1985B-2-144) Irataba Hall, a sports venue in Parker, is also named after him.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''The_Yuma_Daily_Sun''19753-145) In 2002, the US [Bureau of Land Management](/wiki/Bureau_of_Land_Management "Bureau of Land Management") designated 32,745 acres (13,251 ha) of the [Eldorado Mountains](/wiki/Eldorado_Mountains "Eldorado Mountains"), contained largely within the [Lake Mead National Recreation Area](/wiki/Lake_Mead_National_Recreation_Area "Lake Mead National Recreation Area"), as Ireteba Peaks Wilderness.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBureau_of_Land_Management-146)

In March 2015, the Colorado River Indian Tribes celebrated the sesquicentennial history of their reservation with a weekend-long event that included cultural activities and a parade. Speakers at the event stressed the important role that Irataba's strong leadership played in establishing and preserving the reservation, and the continuing need for Native leaders to protect native language and culture. Tribal chairman Dennis Patch commented: "Some people would think we're lucky, as we have this river and 220,000 acre feet of water ... But we have had great leaders, like Chief Irataba. He made sure the Mohaves stayed on land they had lived on since time immemorial."[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGutekunst2015-2) Former CRIT Museum Director Dr. Michael Tsosie stated, "Irataba understood the Mohave people would need to keep fighting to keep what they had." He noted that the Colorado River Indian Reservation "is unique in American history in that they have not lost any land".[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGutekunst2015-2)
